free_text: Elastic scattering and total reaction cross-sections
for the interaction of 12.8 MeV deuterons with 12C,
58Ni, 60 Ni and 209Bi nuclei
same 60Ni(d, 2n), 60Ni(d, n),
58Ni(d, n) data tabulated
The measurements were made using the
12.8 MeV deuteron beam of 120 cm cyclotron of the
Institute of Nuclear Physics in Cracow
The counter, consisting of a thin CsI (Tl)
crystal and EMI- 6097B photomultiplier, was fixed to
the rotatable arm at the distance of 36 cm from the
centre.
The intensity of the deuteron beam was also
monitored by a special scintillation counter viewing
the target at an angle of about 30. degr.
semiconductor detector for alpha-particle
registration
The total beam charge passed during each run was
collected and measured in a Faraday cup placed
between the pole tips of a small permanent magnet.
The Rutherford
scattering on a gold  target was used as a separate
check of the absolute value of the elastic scattering
cross-section.
The yield of neutrons was calculated from the
measurements of the radioactivities induced in
targets by the deuteron bombardment. Beta activities
were measured for 12C, 58Ni and 60Ni targets. In the
case of 209Bi alpha and beta activities were
registered. A Standard Geiger-Mueller counter
technique was used for the beta activity
measurements. Alpha-particles were registered in a
gridded chamber described elsewhere.
The uniformity the targets was checked by means of an
alpha-particle source and a semiconductor detector.
The error of the absolute value of the
scattering cross-section
Tbl. from the ref. NP,49,144,1963

Because of the short half-life time of
the 58Cu isotope it was difficult to measure the
cross-section for the reaction 58Ni(d,2n)58Cu,
therefore the same value of the cross-section as that
of the 60Ni(d,2n)60Cu reaction was adopted.

tbl.5
Using a semiconductor counter as an alpha-particle
detector and very thin bismuth targets 0.1 mg/cm2 it
was possible to resolve the 4.87 MeV alpha particle
group corresponding to the decay of 209Po from the
5.3 MeV alpha particle group of 210Po.
The cross-section for the 209Bi(d,2n) reaction was
calculated from the relative intensity of both
alpha-particle groups.
Self-supporting targets of thicknesses ranging from
0.1 mg/cm2 to 3 mg/cm2. The target was prepared by
evaporation of spectroscopically pure bismuth on a
glass plate covered by a thin film of soap.
tbl.5
The cross-section for the (d,n) reaction was calculated
from measurements of the alpha activity of 2l0Po and
of the beta activity of 210Bi. The result was checked
independently, by calculating the cross-section, using
the measurements of time variation of the alpha-
activity induced in the target.
Self-supporting targets of thicknesses ranging from
0.2 mg/cm2 to 3 mg/cm2. The target was prepared by
evaporation of spectroscopically pure bismuth on a
glass plate covered by a thin film of soap.
tbl.6
Thin bismuth targets 0.2 mg/cm2 evaporated onto
aluminium foil.
The measurements of the cross-section for the (d,3n)
reaction on bismuth were made by following researchers:
Their value 2 mb for 12.8 MeV deuterons was taken into
account in calculations.

tbl.7
tbl.8
The cross-section for the 12C(d,n)13N reaction was
calculated from the measurement of the beta activity
of a 10 min half-life.
The carbon target of the thickness 6.6 mg/cm2 prepared
from the reactor graphite was used for the total
reaction cross-section measurements.
tbl.8
The value for the reaction cross-sections with charged
particle emission was obtained by subtracting the
cross-section for the elastic scattering integrated
over angles larger than 15.degr. from the cross-section
for the emission of all charged particles integrated
over the same range of angles.
The carbon target of the thickness 6.6 mg/cm2 prepared
from the reactor graphite was used for the total
reaction cross-section measurements.
The angular distribution for the elastic scattering
and the absolute value of the elastic scattering
cross-section were taken from:
L.Freindl, etc. in Proc. Int. Rutherford Jubilee Conf.
Manchester (Heyward, London, 1961) p. 529
